title: Vendored datasets
text:
These datasets are vendored here so the code chunks in the cookbook are reproducible. They were originally read from instructor URLs or from local course folders. Raw numeric data of this kind (measurements, textbook problem sets) is generally uncopyrightable fact and these sets are already widely mirrored; each is cited below in good faith with its source and retrieval date.
All files retrieved / collected 2026-06-11.
Task pages read these via a relative path (../../data/<file>); no analysis logic was changed — only the path string.
Organized using Claude Code


Source: Dean, Voss & Draguljic, Design and Analysis of Experiments (2nd ed.), companion data site. Original URL pattern: http://deanvossdraguljic.ietsandbox.net/DeanVossDraguljic/R-data/<file> Retrieved 2026-06-11 (HTTP 200).

title: Fit Simple Linear Regression Model
text:
NoteIntroduction



Fit a simple linear regression to relate a response to one predictor, with coefficient tests, confidence intervals, and predictions.
Grade point average. The director of admissions of a small college selected 120 students at random from the new freshman class in a study to determine whether a student’s grade point average (GPA) at the end of freshman year (Y) can be predicted from the ACT test score (X). The results of the study follow. Assume that first-order regression model (1.1) is appropriate.
# read data
gpa <- read.table("../../data/gpa.txt", header = TRUE)

# fit linear model + print summary
gfit <- lm(GPA ~ ACT, data=gpa)
summary(gfit)


Call:
lm(formula = GPA ~ ACT, data = gpa)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-2.74004 -0.33827  0.04062  0.44064  1.22737 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  2.11405    0.32089   6.588  1.3e-09 ***
ACT          0.03883    0.01277   3.040  0.00292 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.6231 on 118 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.07262,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.06476 
F-statistic:  9.24 on 1 and 118 DF,  p-value: 0.002917

# plot data with fitted regression line
plot(gpa$ACT, gpa$GPA, cex=.5, main="Data and Fitted Regression Model", ylab="Freshman GPA", xlab="ACT Score")
abline(gfit,lwd = 3)







# produce confidence interval for B_1
confint(gfit, 'ACT', level = 0.99)

          0.5 %     99.5 %
ACT 0.005385614 0.07226864

# t-test for relationship
beta0 <- coef(gfit)[1] #coefficient b_0
beta1 <- coef(gfit)[2] #coefficient b_1
fitted_values <- beta0 + beta1 * gpa$ACT #y_hat values

sse <- sum((gpa$GPA - fitted_values) ^2) #sse
mse <- sse / (nrow(gpa)-2) #mse
sigma.hat<-sqrt(mse) #estimated standard dev
sxx <- sum((gpa$ACT - mean(gpa$ACT))^2) #msr
se.beta.hat.1 <- sqrt(mse / sxx) #standard error
T.statistic <- beta1 / se.beta.hat.1 #t statistic
p_value <- 2*pt(T.statistic, df=nrow(gpa)-2, lower.tail=FALSE)
t.star <- qt(.995, nrow(gpa)-2) #t*

# estimate mean y value
newdata <- data.frame(ACT = c(28))
ci <- predict(gfit, newdata, interval = "confidence", level = .95)

# predict y for known x value
pi <- predict(gfit,newdata,interval = "predict", level = .95)

title: Fit Simple Linear Regression Model
text:
Answers

Least Squares Estimates
\(\hat{\beta}_{0} = 2.11405\)
\(\hat{\beta}_{1} = 0.03883\)


Equation of a Line
\(\hat{Y} = 2.11405 + 0.03883X\)


Describe Plot
The estimated regression function appears to fit the data poorly. The majority of the data appears to show a stronger relationship between ACT score and freshman GPA; however, points (29, 0.5) and (31, 1.486) among a handful of others prevent the regression function from having a steeper slope.


Interpret Confidence Interval
If the observational study were repeated again and again, the coefficient for \(\beta_1\) is expected to be in theconfidence interval, calculated with this method, 99% of the time. If the CI included 0, the director of admissions could ignore ACT score as a predictor of Freshman GPA since the results would suggest the observed relationship may be due to error.


Hypothesis Test for Relationship
\(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\)
\(H_a: \beta_1 \neq 0\)
I will be able to reject the null hypothesis if \(T < -2.618137\) or \(T > 2.618137\). The t-statistic is 3.04; therefore, I can reject the null hypothesis.
The P-value is 0.002. The probability of getting a T-statistic of 3.04 would be less than .01 if the null hypothesis were true, satisfying the 99% confidence interval. I can conclude there is a linear association between ACT score and freshman GPA.


Estimate Mean Y
If the observational study were conducted again and again, the mean freshman GPA for a student scoring 28 on the ACT is expected to be in the created confidence interval 95% of the time.


Predict Y
The prediction interval when calculated with this method will contain the student’s actual Freshman GPA 95% of the time.


Note on Difference
The prediction interval includes the prediction of error and will always be wider than the confidence interval, which predicts the mean exclusively.


Additional - Relationship test
If there were no linear association between Y and X, 0 would be included in the confidence interval for slope. However, 0 is not included in the confidence interval, so this conclusion is valid with a significance of 0.05.

title: Transformations
text:
NoteIntroduction



Use a Box-Cox plot to choose a response transformation when constant-variance or normality assumptions are violated.
# read data
sales <- read.table("../../data/CH03PR17.txt", header=TRUE)

# view boxcox plot
gfit <- lm(Y ~ X, data=sales)
library(MASS)
boxcox(gfit)







# transform data
gfit_trfm <- lm(sqrt(Y) ~ X, data=sales)
summary(gfit_trfm)


Call:
lm(formula = sqrt(Y) ~ X, data = sales)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-0.47447 -0.30811  0.01549  0.29541  0.46781 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 10.26093    0.21290   48.20 3.80e-11 ***
X            1.07629    0.03988   26.99 3.83e-09 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.3622 on 8 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9891,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9878 
F-statistic: 728.4 on 1 and 8 DF,  p-value: 3.826e-09

title: Transformations
text:
Answers

Box-Cox Plot
The Box-Cox procedure suggests a lambda = .5 transformation would be appropriate.

For each one-year increase, the expected change in square root of sales in thousands is 1.07629.

title: Indicator Variables
text:
NoteIntroduction



Use an indicator variable to let a single regression capture the shift between two categories (coded 0/1).
# read data: gpa1 (original) + gpa2 (indicator) = gpa (full dataset)
gpa1 <- read.table("../../data/CH01PR19-1.txt", header=TRUE)
gpa2 <- read.table("../../data/CH08PR16.txt", header=TRUE)
gpa <- cbind(gpa1, gpa2[1]) #combine

# fit linear model
gfit <- lm(GPA ~ ACT + Indicator, data=gpa)
summary(gfit)


Call:
lm(formula = GPA ~ ACT + Indicator, data = gpa)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-2.70304 -0.35574  0.02541  0.45747  1.25037 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  2.19842    0.33886   6.488 2.18e-09 ***
ACT          0.03789    0.01285   2.949  0.00385 ** 
Indicator   -0.09430    0.11997  -0.786  0.43341    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.6241 on 117 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.07749,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.06172 
F-statistic: 4.914 on 2 and 117 DF,  p-value: 0.008928

# confidence interval for beta_2
qt <- qt(.975, 117)
ll <- -.0943 - (qt* .11997)
ul <- -.0943 + (qt* .11997)
The 95% confidence interval for \(\beta_2\) is (-0.3318943, 0.1432943). We fail to reject the null hypothesis, because 0 is contained in the confidence interval. This suggests there is no value added by including \(X_2\) in the model.
# plot residuals against variable; color by indicator
gpa.e <- resid(gfit)
palette(c("gray", "red"))
test <- gpa$Indicator + 1
plot(gpa$ACT, gpa.e, col=test, pch=18,
     main="Residual Plot Against ACT Score Separated by Indicator",
     xlab="ACT Score", ylab="Residuals")

title: Fit Piecewise Linear Model
text:
NoteIntroduction



Fit a piecewise linear model when the relationship changes slope at a known breakpoint.
# read data
properties <- read.table("../../data/CH06PR18-1.txt", header=TRUE)

# center ages variable (square of variable included in model)
Centered_Ages <- properties$Age-mean(properties$Age)
Sq_Centered_Ages <- Centered_Ages^2

# create table
prop <- cbind(properties, Centered_Ages, Sq_Centered_Ages)

# plot scatter with vertical line at break
plot(prop$Age, prop$Rental_Rates, pch=18, col="red", ylab="Rental Rates", xlab="Property Age", main="Regression on X1")
abline(v=10, lty=2)

# fit piecewise model
piecewise.fit <- lm(Rental_Rates ~ Age + pmax(0, Age - 10) + I(Age > 10), data=prop)

# generate x variables for prediction
a.grid <- seq(0, max(prop$Age), length.out=1000)

# generate piecewise prediction lines
predictions <- predict(piecewise.fit, newdata=data.frame(Age = a.grid), interval = "confidence")

# add lines to graph (run entire chunk for success)
lines(x=a.grid, y=predictions[,1], col="gray3")

title: Check Assumptions
text:
NoteIntroduction



Run these residual plots and tests to verify the core linear-model assumptions before trusting any inference from the fit.
Assumptions to check:
# read data
gpa <- read.table("../../data/CH03PR03.txt", header=TRUE)

# plot data
plot(gpa$X,gpa$Y, ylab="Freshman GPA", xlab="ACT Score", main="Scatter Plot", pch=18, col="orange")







# plot normality
hist(gpa$X, xlab="ACT Scores", ylab="Frequency of Score", main="Histogram", col="orange")







# plot residuals
gfit <- lm(Y ~ X, data=gpa) #fit linear model
gpa.e <- resid(gfit) #residuals
plot(gpa$X, gpa.e, xlab="ACT Score", ylab="Residual", main="Residual Plot", col="orange", pch=18)
abline(h=0) #horizontal line
abline(h=1, lty=c(2)) #dotted line
abline(h=-1, lty=c(2)) #dotted line







# plot QQ plot
library(car)
qqPlot(rstandard(gfit), col="orange", pch=18)








[1]   9 115

# test normality (reject if < 0.05)
shapiro.test(gpa.e)


    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  gpa.e
W = 0.95249, p-value = 0.0003304
# plot residuals against unused variables
plot(gpa$V2, gpa.e, col="orange", pch=18, xlab="V2", ylab="Residuals", main="V2 Residual Plot")
abline(h=0)







plot(gpa$V3, gpa.e, col="orange", pch=18, xlab="V3", ylab="Residuals", main="V3 Residual Plot")
abline(h=0)

title: Simple Logistic Regression
text:
NoteIntroduction



Use simple logistic regression to model a binary outcome from a single predictor, reading the slope as an odds ratio.
# plot logistic model with b0 = 20, b1 = -.2
X <- cbind(1:200)
pi_1a <- exp(20-.2*X)/(1+exp(20-.2*X))
plot(X, pi_1a, pch=18, col="slateblue1", main="Logistic Mean Response with Given Parameters")







# odds ratio is approximately exp(b1)
odds126 <- exp(20-.2*126)/(1+exp(20-.2*126))
odds125 <- exp(20-.2*125)/(1+exp(20-.2*125))
ratio <- odds126/odds125
exp(-.2)

[1] 0.8187308

# read data
dues <- read.table("../../data/CH14PR07.txt", header=TRUE)

# fit logistic model
gfit2 <- glm(Renewal ~ Increase, data=dues, family = binomial(link ="logit"))
summary(gfit2)


Call:
glm(formula = Renewal ~ Increase, family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
    data = dues)

Deviance Residuals: 
    Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max  
-1.7651  -1.0012   0.6502   0.9828   1.6455  

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)  
(Intercept) -4.80751    2.65576  -1.810   0.0703 .
Increase     0.12508    0.06676   1.874   0.0610 .
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 41.455  on 29  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 37.465  on 28  degrees of freedom
AIC: 41.465

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4
The fitted response function is \[\hat{\pi} = \frac{\exp(-4.80751 + 0.12508X)}{1+\exp(-4.80751 + 0.12508X)}\]
# generate prediction curve
predIncrease <- seq(0,100, by=1)                    # create x values
logit.hat2 <- predict.glm(gfit2,                    # ???
                          newdata = data.frame(Increase=predIncrease),
                          se.fit=TRUE)
prob.hat2 <- exp(logit.hat2$fit)/(1+exp(logit.hat2$fit)) # logistic equation

# plot original data
plot(dues$Increase,
     dues$Renewal,
     pch=18, col="slateblue1",
     main="Annual Dues Increase",
     ylab="Renewal Selection",
     xlab="Increase Amount")

# plot loess smoothed line
loess2 <- loess.smooth(dues$Increase, dues$Renewal)
lines(loess2, col="slateblue2")                     # loess smooth line
lines(predIncrease, prob.hat2, col="red")           # predicted values line







# calculate and interpret odds ratio
beta_1 <- exp(gfit2$coefficients[2])
The value is 1.133237 and suggests that for each additional dollar dues increase, the odds of a member choosing not to renew their dues increases 13%.
dues_b <- gfit2$coefficients                        # save b1 and b0

# estimated probability that members will not renew if the dues increase $40
pi_2d <- exp(dues_b[1]+dues_b[2]*40)/(1+exp(dues_b[1]+dues_b[2]*40))

# dues increase for which 75 percent of the members are expected not to renew
Y <- exp(dues_b[1]+dues_b[2]*X)/(1+exp(dues_b[1]+dues_b[2]*X)) # equation
answer <- X[which(Y > .74 & Y < 0.76)]              # find Y = .75

title: Fit Multiple Linear Regression Model
text:
NoteIntroduction



Fit a multiple linear regression to relate a response to several predictors at once, then check residuals and test the coefficients.
# read data
patient <- read.table("../../data/CH06PR15.txt", header=TRUE)

# scatter plot matrix
pairs(patient, col="slategray4")







# correlation matrix
cor(patient)

             Satisfaction        Age   Severity    Anxiety
Satisfaction    1.0000000 -0.7867555 -0.6029417 -0.6445910
Age            -0.7867555  1.0000000  0.5679505  0.5696775
Severity       -0.6029417  0.5679505  1.0000000  0.6705287
Anxiety        -0.6445910  0.5696775  0.6705287  1.0000000

# fit linear model
gfit <- lm(Satisfaction ~ Age + Severity + Anxiety, data=patient)

# plot residuals against fitted values
patient.e <- resid(gfit)
plot(gfit$fitted.values, patient.e, col="slategray4", pch=18)







# plot against single variables
par(mfrow=c(2,2))
plot(patient$Age, patient.e, col="slategray4", pch=18)
plot(patient$Severity, patient.e, col="slategray4", pch=18)
plot(patient$Anxiety, patient.e, col="slategray4", pch=18)

# plot against interaction variables
plot(patient$Age*patient$Severity, patient.e, col="slategray4", pch=18)







plot(patient$Age*patient$Anxiety, patient.e, col="slategray4", pch=18)
plot(patient$Severity*patient$Anxiety, patient.e, col="slategray4", pch=18)

# f test for not all coefficients = 0
summary(gfit)

title: Fit Multiple Linear Regression Model
text:
Answers

F Test for all Coefficients
The alternatives are as follows:
\(H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta_3 = 0\)
\(H_a:\) not all \(\beta_k = 0\)
If the f-statistic is greater than F*, I will reject the null hypothesis. If it is less than or equal to 2.2190586, I will fail to reject the null hypothesis.
Because the f-statistic is greater than the f-value, I can reject the null hypothesis and conclude that at least one of the beta terms is not equal to zero.
The p-value is 1.542e-10, which makes sense as it is less than .10, which was the required level of significance in order to reject the null.


Coefficient of Multiple Determination
The coefficient of multiple determination indicates that the inclusion of these three predictor variables reduces the variability in Y by 68.21973%.


F Test for B_3
\(H_0: \beta_3 = 0\)
\(H_a: \beta_3 \neq 0\)
Because F-statistic is NOT greater than F*, I fail to reject the null hypothesis. The P-value is 0.06468, which does not meet a significance level of .025.
The degree of marginal linear association between \(Y\) and \(X_1\) is reduced for each predictor variable considered before \(X_1\) is added. This makes sense because the reduction in variability of Y has the opportunity to be attributed to other variables first and will not be double-counted.
\(SSR(X_2 \mid X_1, X_3)\) has 1 DF.

title: Cross-Validation
text:
NoteIntroduction



Use k-fold cross-validation to estimate prediction error and choose model complexity by the one-standard-error rule.
pacman::p_load(dplyr, rsample, tidyverse)

# read data
electemp <- read_csv("../../data/electemp.csv")
cv <- vfold_cv(electemp, v = 10)
The dataframe cv has ten rows, each of which holds information about the dataset which has been split into training and test data. The first column holds this information and the second column informs us which fold (1-10) that dataset corresponds with.
cv2 <- cv %>%
    rowwise() %>%
    mutate(fit = list(lm(usage ~ poly(temp, 2), data = splits)))
By wrapping lm() in list(), we can preserve a rectangular data frame where the multiple outputs of lm() are accessible in the row from which their data was drawn. Recall data is stored in splits - information for a dataset is stored within first element of row.
The dataframe remains rowwise because we are using the mutate function, which expands the rows rather than collapses them.
train_and_test <- function(splits, sp) {
  fit <- lm(usage ~ poly(temp, sp), data = splits)
  test <- assessment(splits)
  test <- mutate(test, predicted = predict(fit, newdata = test))
  summarize(test, mse = mean( (usage - predicted)^2 ))$mse
}
mcv <- cv2 %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(mse = train_and_test(splits, 2))
cv_result <- mcv %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    summarize(se = sd(mse) / sqrt(n()), mse = mean(mse))
cv_result

# A tibble: 1 × 2
     se   mse
  <dbl> <dbl>
1  26.0  98.3
We need to use ungroup so that the dataframe will not treat each row as its own group. This allows summarize to collapse across all rows, rather than computing the values rowwise.
cv <- vfold_cv(electemp, v = 10) %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  summarize(mse = train_and_test(splits, 2)) %>%
  summarize(se = sd(mse) / sqrt(n()), mse = mean(mse))
models <- tibble(degree = 1:7)
cv <- vfold_cv(electemp, k = 10)
mcv <- crossing(cv, models)
The dataframe mcv offers every combination of the folds (1-10) and the number of polynomials fit in the model (1-7).
cv_result <- mcv %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(mse = train_and_test(splits, degree))
cv_result <- cv_result %>%
  group_by(degree) %>%
  summarize(se = sd(mse) / sqrt(n()), mse = mean(mse))
cv_result

# A tibble: 7 × 3

title: Permutation Tests
text:
NoteIntroduction



Use a permutation test to build a null distribution by shuffling group labels — no parametric assumptions needed.
pacman::p_load(ggplot2, tidyverse)

# read data
rats <- read_csv('../../data/ozonerats.csv')

# plot data boxplots
ggplot(rats) + geom_boxplot(aes(group, weightgain))
str(rats)

spec_tbl_df [45 × 2] (S3: spec_tbl_df/tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
 $ weightgain: num [1:45] 41 38.4 24.9 25.9 21.9 18.3 13.1 27.3 28.5 -16.9 ...
 $ group     : chr [1:45] "control" "control" "control" "control" ...
 - attr(*, "spec")=
  .. cols(
  ..   weightgain = col_double(),
  ..   group = col_character()
  .. )
 - attr(*, "problems")=<externalptr>
The dataframe has two variables: weightgain, a numeric column with rats’ weights; and group, a character column with which group each rat belonged to.
\(H_0: F(x) = G(x)\)
where \(X_1,...X_n \sim^{iid} F(x)\) and \(Y_1, ..., Y_m \sim^{iid} G(x)\)
\(H_a: \mu_{F(x)} \neq \mu_{G(x)}\)
t0 <- summarise(rats,
                t_stat = abs(t.test(weightgain ~ group)$statistic))

df_perm <- map(1:1e3,
               function(x) mutate(rats,
                                  group = sample(group)))
t_perm <- map_dfr(df_perm,
                  summarize,
                  t_stat = abs(t.test(weightgain ~ group)$statistic))

p_value <- mean(c(t0$t_stat, t_perm$t_stat) >= t0$t_stat)
ggplot(t_perm)+
  geom_histogram(aes(t_stat, fill = t_stat >= t0$t_stat)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = t0$t_stat) +
  ggtitle("Permutation null", subtitle = paste("empirical p-value = ", p_value))
The rejection region is one-sided, because I’m interested in how unlikely it was to achieve my t-statistic with the data shuffled.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Interval
text:
NoteIntroduction



Resample with replacement to form a pivotal bootstrap confidence interval for a statistic without distributional assumptions.
pacman::p_load(readr, dplyr)

# fish data + N_hat carried over from the Bootstrap Estimate task
fish <- read_table('../../data/fish.txt')
N_hat <- function(df) {
  bh_lm <- lm(I(1/R) ~ I(1/S), data = df)
  beta1 <- coef(bh_lm)[1]
  beta2 <- coef(bh_lm)[2]
  (1-beta2)/ beta1
}

# set confidence level
alpha <- .05

# set number of bootstrap samples
B <- 1000

# bootstrap for CI
boot_pivot <- replicate(B, {
  df_boot <- slice_sample(fish, n = nrow(fish), replace = TRUE)
  N_hat(df_boot) - N_hat(fish)
})

# Use setNames to replace the names that quantile() gives its output
q <- setNames(quantile(boot_pivot, c(1 - alpha/2, alpha/2)), NULL)
confidence_interval <- N_hat(fish) - q

# print confidence interval
confidence_interval

title: Choosing Contrasts
text:
NoteIntroduction



Set up contrasts to express the specific treatment comparisons you care about — main effects, pairwise differences, or interactions.
The experiment was run to compare the effects of auditory and visual cues on speed of response of a human subject. The experimenters were interested in the effects on the subjects’ reaction time of the auditory and visual cues and also in different elapsed times between cue and stimulus. Thus, there were two different treatment factors: “cue stimulus” at two levels “auditory” or “visual,” and “elapsed time between cue and stimulus” at three levels “five,” “ten,” or “fifteen” seconds. This gave a total of six treatment combinations.
Comparison: Auditory vs. Visual \[\gamma = \tfrac{1}{3}(\tau_1 + \tau_2 + \tau_3) - \tfrac{1}{3}(\tau_4 + \tau_5 + \tau_6)\]
Comparison: 5 sec vs. 10 sec Before Cue \[\gamma = \tfrac{1}{2}(\tau_1 + \tau_4) - \tfrac{1}{2}(\tau_2 + \tau_5)\]
Comparison: 5 sec vs. 15 sec Before Cue \[\gamma = \tfrac{1}{2}(\tau_1 + \tau_4) - \tfrac{1}{2}(\tau_3 + \tau_6)\]
Comparison: 10 sec vs. 15 sec Before Cue \[\gamma = \tfrac{1}{2}(\tau_2 + \tau_5) - \tfrac{1}{2}(\tau_3 + \tau_6)\]
Burt Beiter, Doug Fairchild, Leo Russo, and Jim Wirtley, in 1990, ran an experiment to compare the relative strengths of two similarly priced brands of paper towel under varying levels of moisture saturation and liquid type. The treatment factors were “amount of liquid” (factor A, with levels 5 and 10 drops coded 1 and 2), “brand of towel” (factor B, with levels coded 1 and 2), and “type of liquid” (factor C, with levels “beer” and “water” coded 1 and 2). A 2 × 2 × 2 factorial experiment with r = 3 was run in a completely randomized design.
List all treatment contrasts that are likely to be of primary interest to the experimenters.
Tukey Multiplier: \[w_T = q_{v,\,n-v,\,\alpha} / \sqrt{2}\]
Bonferroni Multiplier: \[w_B = t_{n-v,\,\alpha/(2m)}\]

title: Assign Random Treatments
text:
NoteIntroduction



Use this to randomly assign experimental units across treatment levels so each treatment receives its planned number of replicates.
Suppose that you are planning to run an experiment with one treatment factor having four levels and no blocking factors. Suppose that the calculation of the required number of observations has given r1 = r2 = r3 = r4 = 5. Assign at random 20 experimental units to the v = 4 levels of the treatments, so that each treatment is assigned 5 units.
r <- c(5,5,5,5)                                         #set # replicates
v <- length(r)                                          #set # treatments
n <- sum(r)                                             #set n
unsorted_trtmts = rep(1:4, r)                           #create r reps of v treatments
data = data.frame(unsorted_trtmts=unsorted_trtmts)                    #make data frame

set.seed(12345)                                         #set seed
unsorted_rand <- runif(n)                               #random deviates of uniform distr.
data_sort = data.frame(data,                            #add column with random numbers
                       unsorted.rn = round(unsorted_rand, 3))

sorted_rand = sort(unsorted_rand)                       #sort by random numbers
ordered_rand = order(unsorted_rand)                     #assign random order
sort_trtmts = unsorted_trtmts[ordered_rand]             #random order treatments
data = data.frame(data,
                  sort_trtmts = sort_trtmts,            #column sorted treatments
                  sorted_rand = sorted_rand,            #column sorted random numbers
                  exper_unit = seq(1:20))               #number experimental units

title: Fit 2-Way Complete Model
text:
NoteIntroduction



Fit a two-way complete model with both main effects and their interaction, then test which terms to retain.
# read data
paperTowel <-
  read.table(
    "../../data/paper.towel.strength.txt",
    header = TRUE
  )

# consider as factors
paperTowel$A <- as.factor(paperTowel$A)
paperTowel$B <- as.factor(paperTowel$B)
paperTowel$C <- as.factor(paperTowel$C)

# fit model including AB interaction
model7.8 <- aov(strength ~ A + B + C + A*B, data=paperTowel)
\(H_0\): An effect is insignificant to the model
\(H_a\): An effect is not insignificant and should be added to the model
I will use the ANOVA table to determine P-values found via F testing.
Decision Rule: Any p-value under 0.01 will suggest that the corresponding effect is significant and should be maintained in the model. I will use 0.01, so that after the four tests, the end result will have a confidence of 0.04 as desired in the problem statement.
# create anova table
drop1(model7.8, ~., test="F")

title: Test Treatment Effect
text:
NoteIntroduction



Fit a one-way model and use ANOVA to test whether the treatment has any effect on the response.
pacman::p_load(dplyr, ggplot2, lsmeans)

# load data
trout <- read.table("../../data/trout.txt", header=TRUE)
trout$Sulfa <- as.factor(trout$Sulfa)                   #consider sulfa as treatments

ggplot(trout, aes(x = Sulfa,                            #view boxplots of sulfa groups
                  y = Hemo,
                  group=Sulfa)) +
  geom_boxplot()







model <- lm(Hemo ~ Sulfa, data=trout)                   #fit linear model
lsmeans(model, "Sulfa")                                 #find least squares means

Confidence level used: 0.95 

#mark least squares means on boxplot
ggplot(trout, aes(x = Sulfa, y = Hemo, group=Sulfa)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(aes(x=1, y=7.2, col="red"), pch=8, lwd=3) +

Residuals 36 56.471  1.5686

title: Test Treatment Effect
text:
Answers

Suitable Model
\[Y_{it} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{it}\]
for \(i=1,2,3,4\) and \(t=1,\dots,10\) where \(Y_{it}\) = the grams of hemoglobin per 100mL, \(\mu + \tau_i\) = the mean response for each treatment, and \(\epsilon_{it}\) = the error terms which are approx. \(N(0, \sigma^2)\).


F Test - Effect of Sulfa
\(H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \tau_3 = \tau_4\)
\(H_a:\) not all \(\tau_i\) are equal
\(\alpha = .05\)
The p value is 0.002685. Because the p value is less than the significance level, I can reject the null hypothesis and conclude that at least two treatment groups have unequal tau_i.
I will not keep the model that considers all treatments equal and instead adopt a model that suggests sulfamerazine does have an effect on hemoglobin content.

title: Check Normality
text:
NoteIntroduction



Check whether multivariate data are normal — variable by variable and jointly — before applying methods that assume it.
pacman::p_load(dplyr, car)

# read data
psych <-read.table("../../data/T4-6.DAT") %>%
  rename(Indep = V1,
         Supp = V2,
         Benev = V3,
         Conform = V4,
         Leader = V5,
         Gender = V6,
         Socio = V7)

psych <- psych[,1:5]

# check univariate normality
par(mfrow = c(3,2))
hist(psych$Indep,
     main = "Independent",
     col = "cadetblue2")
hist(psych$Supp,
     main = "Support",
     col = "cadetblue2")
hist(psych$Benev,
     main = "Benevolence",
     col = "cadetblue2")
hist(psych$Conform,
     main = "Conformity",
     col = "cadetblue2")
hist(psych$Leader,
     main = "Leadership",
     col = "cadetblue2")

par(mfrow = c(2,3))







qqPlot(psych$Indep)

[1] 111  55

qqPlot(psych$Supp)

[1] 60 30

qqPlot(psych$Benev)

[1] 104 111

qqPlot(psych$Conform)

[1] 83 73

qqPlot(psych$Leader)

[1] 127  60

# check multivariate normality
dev.mat <- scale(psych, center=T, scale=F)        # deviation matrix
S <- cov(psych)
S.inv <- solve(S)                                 # inverse of S

d2 <- rep(0,130)                                  # initialize d2 with zero
for(i in 1:130){
    d2[i] <- t(dev.mat[i,]) %*% S.inv %*% dev.mat[i,] # d_i^2
    }

plot(qchisq(((1:130)-.5)/130, 5), asp = 1,        # theoretical quantiles of chi^2
     sort(d2), type="n",
     xlab = "Quantile of Chi-square with df = 5",
     ylab = "d-square",
     xlim = c(0,20), ylim = c(0,20)
     )
points(qchisq(((1:130)-.5)/130, 5), sort(d2), pch=16, col=2, cex = .75)
abline(v = 0, col="grey")
abline(0, 1, col="grey")







qchisq(.5, 5)

[1] 4.35146
Because the points hug the line with slope of 1 through the origin and approximately half of the values are below 4.35 (\(q_{c,p}(.5)\)), I believe this dataset has multivariate normality.

title: Forecasting
text:
NoteIntroduction



Use a fitted ARMA model to forecast future values and draw 95% prediction bounds.
# detrend + deseasonalize the per-day oil series (carried over)
oil <- scan("../../data/US_oil_production.txt")
d <- 12
n <- length(oil)
filt <- c(0.5, rep(1, 5*d-1), 0.5)/(5*d)            # 5-year MA window
per.day <- oil / c(31,28.25,31,30,31,30,31,31,30,31,30,31)
longer.per.day <- c(per.day[(5*d/2):1], per.day, per.day[n:(n-(5*d/2)+1)])
per.day.MA <- filter(longer.per.day, filt)[(5*d/2+1):(5*d/2+n)]
per.day.MA.resids <- per.day - per.day.MA
per.day.matrix <- matrix(per.day.MA.resids, nrow=d)
s.hat.per.day <- round(rowMeans(per.day.matrix) - mean(rowMeans(per.day.matrix)))
per.day.ds <- per.day.MA.resids - s.hat.per.day
# fit the ARMA(1,1) model selected in the Fit ARIMA Model task
model <- arima(per.day.ds, order = c(1,0,1))

# forecast the next h steps
h <- 24
model.forecast <- predict(model, n.ahead = h)

# 95% prediction bounds
confidence.level <- 1.96
upper <- model.forecast$pred + confidence.level * model.forecast$se
lower <- model.forecast$pred - confidence.level * model.forecast$se

# plot the history followed by the forecast and its bounds
t_hist   <- seq_along(per.day.ds)
t_future <- length(per.day.ds) + (1:h)

plot(t_hist, per.day.ds, type = "l",
     xlim = c(0, max(t_future)),
     ylim = range(per.day.ds, upper, lower),
     xlab = "time index (months)", ylab = "deseasonalized per-day oil")
lines(t_future, model.forecast$pred, col = "red")           # point forecast
lines(t_future, upper, col = "blue", lty = 2)               # upper bound
lines(t_future, lower, col = "blue", lty = 2)               # lower bound
abline(h = 0, col = "grey")







# table of forecast values with bounds
forecast.interval <- data.frame(step = 1:h,
                                lower = lower,
                                forecast = model.forecast$pred,
                                upper = upper)
head(forecast.interval)

  step      lower  forecast    upper
1    1  -10.94427 234.76275 480.4698
2    2  -98.73920 187.80453 474.3483
3    3 -159.28354 150.39580 460.0751
4    4 -202.91033 120.59456 444.0995
5    5 -235.12678  96.85374 428.8343
6    6 -259.30808  77.94089 415.1899

title: Detrending
text:
NoteIntroduction



Remove a series’ trend — by fitting a polynomial or by differencing — to move it toward stationarity.
A time series of wind speed (excluding gusts) was recorded every twenty minutes at an airport in Fort Lauderdale during Hurricane Wilma in October 2005, analyzed on the square root scale.
winds <- c(2.90, 3.06, 3.62, 3.38, 3.43, 3.07, 3.13, 3.27, 3.35, 3.21, 3.26,
           3.51, 3.21, 2.83, 2.59, 2.62, 2.88, 2.75, 2.52, 2.68, 3.13, 3.22,

           3.77, 3.79, 3.80, 3.96, 3.87, 3.68, 4.01, 4.26, 4.16, 4.32, 3.93,
           3.86, 3.79, 3.89, 4.04, 3.46, 3.26, 3.17, 3.41, 3.67, 3.68, 3.88,
           3.84, 4.05, 4.10, 3.59, 3.75, 4.00, 3.67, 4.02, 4.35, 4.09, 4.03,
           3.99, 4.13, 4.15, 4.28, 4.51, 4.54, 4.44, 4.51, 4.50, 4.47, 4.52,
           4.43, 4.93, 5.15, 4.90, 5.09, 4.95, 5.24, 5.67, 5.69, 6.12, 6.30,
           6.25, 6.47, 6.63, 6.48, 6.41, 6.58, 6.97, 7.62, 8.03, 7.98, 7.67,
           7.26, 7.51, 7.65, 7.57, 7.50, 7.86, 7.85, 7.27, 6.77, 6.81, 6.83,
           6.64, 6.49, 6.27, 6.07, 5.96, 5.64, 5.52, 5.45, 5.37, 5.18, 4.89,
           4.53, 4.31, 4.37, 4.41, 4.54, 4.68, 4.80, 4.59, 4.42, 4.14, 4.00,
           4.17, 3.95, 3.97, 3.95, 3.89, 3.97)

hours <- seq(from=0, by=1/3, length=length(winds))

## plot the time series
plot(hours, winds, type="l",
     xlab="hours since midnight, 23 Oct 2005",
     ylab="square root of wind speed")
Remove the trend by fitting a polynomial model with least squares, then inspecting the residual series.
## Now fit a polynomial model to the data using least squares.
lm.winds <- lm(winds ~ hours + I(hours^2) + I(hours^3))

## Summarize the model obtained.
summary(lm.winds)


Call:
lm(formula = winds ~ hours + I(hours^2) + I(hours^3))

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-1.1516 -0.5169 -0.1011  0.4489  1.7242 

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  3.934e+00  2.262e-01  17.390  < 2e-16 ***
hours       -2.973e-01  4.305e-02  -6.905 1.83e-10 ***
I(hours^2)   2.412e-02  2.195e-03  10.988  < 2e-16 ***
I(hours^3)  -3.911e-04  3.159e-05 -12.380  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.6824 on 134 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7707,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7656 
F-statistic: 150.1 on 3 and 134 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

par(mfrow=c(1,2))
## Plot the series and the regression line.
plot(hours, winds, type="l",
     xlab="hours since midnight, 23 Oct 2005",
     ylab="square root of wind speed")
lines(hours, fitted(lm.winds))

## Plot the residual series with a horizontal line at y=0.
plot(hours, resid(lm.winds), type="l",
     xlab="hours since midnight, 23 Oct 2005",
     ylab="residuals")
abline(h=0)
Differencing is an alternative way to remove trend.
## Difference the time series
diff.winds <- diff(winds)

par(mfrow=c(1,2))
## Plot the differenced series, correcting the time scale.
plot(hours[-1], diff.winds, type="l",
     xlab="hours since midnight, 23 Oct 2005",
     ylab="1st diff. of sqrt wind speed")

## We difference the time series again!
diff2.winds <- diff(diff.winds)

## Plot the twice differenced series, again correcting the time scale.
plot(hours[-(1:2)], diff2.winds, type="l",
     xlab="hours since midnight, 23 Oct 2005",
     ylab="2nd diff. of sqrt wind speed")

title: Fit ARIMA Model
text:
NoteIntroduction



Fit an ARMA model to a stationary series, then drop parameters whose confidence intervals contain zero to simplify it.
Here the model is fit to the detrended, deseasonalized U.S. per-day oil production series with the arima function; preprocessing is carried over from the Detrending / Deseasoning tasks.
library(kableExtra)

# detrend + deseasonalize the per-day oil series (carried over)
oil <- scan("../../data/US_oil_production.txt")
years <- seq(from=1980, by=1/12, length=length(oil))
d <- 12
n <- length(oil)
filt <- c(0.5, rep(1, 5*d-1), 0.5)/(5*d)            # 5-year MA window
per.day <- oil / c(31,28.25,31,30,31,30,31,31,30,31,30,31)
longer.per.day <- c(per.day[(5*d/2):1], per.day, per.day[n:(n-(5*d/2)+1)])
per.day.MA <- filter(longer.per.day, filt)[(5*d/2+1):(5*d/2+n)]
per.day.MA.resids <- per.day - per.day.MA
per.day.matrix <- matrix(per.day.MA.resids, nrow=d)
s.hat.per.day <- round(rowMeans(per.day.matrix) - mean(rowMeans(per.day.matrix)))
per.day.ds <- per.day.MA.resids - s.hat.per.day
Start with an ARMA(2,1) model.
arma21.model <- arima(per.day.ds, order=c(2,0,1))

arma21.resids <- resid(arma21.model)

par(mfrow=c(1,1), cex=0.7, bty="L", mar=c(4,4,1.5,1))
plot(years, arma21.resids, type="l", xlab="year", ylab="ARMA21 residuals")







arma21.model


Call:
arima(x = per.day.ds, order = c(2, 0, 1))

Coefficients:
         ar1      ar2      ma1  intercept
      1.1064  -0.2234  -0.4990     4.9656
s.e.  0.2023   0.1529   0.1892    27.9281

sigma^2 estimated as 15635:  log likelihood = -2249.48,  aic = 4508.97

phi1.lower <- 1.1064-(1.96*.2023)
phi2.lower <- -0.2234-(1.96*.1529)
theta1.lower <- -.4990-(1.96*.1892)

phi1.upper <- 1.1064+(1.96*.2023)
phi2.upper <- -0.2234+(1.96*.1529)
theta1.upper <- -.4990+(1.96*.1892)

parameter.table <- data.frame("estimate" = c("phi_1", "phi_2", "theta_1"),
                              "lower limit" = c(phi1.lower, phi2.lower, theta1.lower),
                              "upper limit" = c(phi1.upper, phi2.upper, theta1.upper))
The model is described by the equation:
\[X_t - \phi_1X_{t-1} - \phi_2X_{t-2} = Z_t + \theta_1Z_{t-1}\]
where \(X_t\) is a random variable, \(\phi_1\) is 1.1064, \(\phi_2\) is -0.2234, \(\theta_1\) is -0.4990, and \(Z_t\) has the distribution WN(0, 15635). The confidence intervals below can be interpreted as: should we estimate the parameters with this method again and again, we expect the true parameters to fall within the calculated intervals 95% of the time.
kable(parameter.table) %>%
  kable_styling(font_size = 9)




estimate
lower.limit
upper.limit




phi_1
0.709892
1.502908


phi_2
-0.523084
0.076284


theta_1
-0.869832
-0.128168
Because the confidence interval for \(\phi_2\) contains zero, we do not believe it is significant in the model and it can be dropped. Proceed with an ARMA(1,1) process instead:
\[X_t - \phi_1X_{t-1} = Z_t + \theta_1Z_{t-1}\]
arma11.model <- arima(per.day.ds, order=c(1,0,1))
arma11.resids <- resid(arma11.model)

arma11.model


Call:
arima(x = per.day.ds, order = c(1, 0, 1))

Coefficients:
         ar1      ma1  intercept
      0.7966  -0.1966     3.8526
s.e.  0.0493   0.0852    25.8678

sigma^2 estimated as 15715:  log likelihood = -2250.4,  aic = 4508.8

phi1.lower <- 0.7966-(1.96*0.0493)
theta1.lower <- -0.1966-(1.96*0.0852)

phi1.upper <- 0.7966+(1.96*0.0493)
theta1.upper <- -0.1966+(1.96*0.0852)

parameter.table2 <- data.frame("estimate" = c("phi_1", "theta_1"),
                              "lower limit" = c(phi1.lower, theta1.lower),
                              "upper limit" = c(phi1.upper, theta1.upper))

kable(parameter.table2) %>%
  kable_styling(font_size = 9)




estimate
lower.limit
upper.limit




phi_1
0.699972
0.893228


theta_1
-0.363592

title: ACF and PACF
text:
NoteIntroduction



Read the sample ACF and PACF to choose candidate AR and MA orders for a stationary series.
Here the functions are applied to the detrended, deseasonalized U.S. per-day oil production series (the preprocessing is carried over from the Detrending / Deseasoning tasks).
# detrend + deseasonalize the per-day oil series (carried over)
oil <- scan("../../data/US_oil_production.txt")
years <- seq(from=1980, by=1/12, length=length(oil))
d <- 12
n <- length(oil)
filt <- c(0.5, rep(1, 5*d-1), 0.5)/(5*d)            # 5-year MA window
per.day <- oil / c(31,28.25,31,30,31,30,31,31,30,31,30,31)
longer.per.day <- c(per.day[(5*d/2):1], per.day, per.day[n:(n-(5*d/2)+1)])
per.day.MA <- filter(longer.per.day, filt)[(5*d/2+1):(5*d/2+n)]
per.day.MA.resids <- per.day - per.day.MA
per.day.matrix <- matrix(per.day.MA.resids, nrow=d)
s.hat.per.day <- round(rowMeans(per.day.matrix) - mean(rowMeans(per.day.matrix)))
per.day.ds <- per.day.MA.resids - s.hat.per.day
par(mfrow=c(2,2))
plot(years, per.day.ds, type="l", main="Time Series")
acf(per.day.ds, lag.max=60, main="Sample ACF")
pacf(per.day.ds, lag.max=60, main="Sample PACF")

par(mfrow=c(2,2))







hist(per.day.ds)
qqnorm(per.day.ds)
qqline(per.day.ds)

Box.test(per.day.ds, lag = 60, type="Ljung-Box")


    Box-Ljung test

data:  per.day.ds
X-squared = 738.1, df = 60, p-value < 2.2e-16
The ACF decays more slowly than the PACF, suggesting an AR component; the PACF has values outside the bounds at lags 1 and 2, pointing toward a low-order ARMA model. The Ljung-Box test rejects IID noise, so a model for the errors is warranted.

title: Check Assumptions
text:
NoteIntroduction



After detrending and deseasonalizing, check that the residual (noise) process looks stationary, IID, and normal.
This continues the Columbus monthly temperature decomposition from the Deseasoning task.
temp <-
  c(29.9, 28.1, 44.3, 50.6, 61.0, 72.4, 75.6, 76.9, 63.6, 55.7, 38.1, 29.2,
    27.9, 30.7, 34.4, 50.5, 60.6, 70.8, 73.9, 73.6, 65.8, 55.5, 37.3, 37.4,
    28.0, 35.9, 42.9, 47.9, 57.1, 70.0, 74.3, 70.4, 65.4, 55.3, 40.6, 35.4,
    37.0, 39.4, 42.9, 53.7, 67.2, 71.7, 74.9, 75.7, 71.5, 56.0, 46.0, 41.5,
    31.3, 36.2, 36.9, 54.7, 64.8, 74.5, 79.2, 73.7, 68.3, 54.7, 48.6, 34.6,
    28.0, 36.5, 46.5, 50.5, 64.5, 71.7, 71.9, 71.3, 65.8, 57.5, 42.1, 24.4,
    27.6, 35.9, 37.1, 55.8, 63.3, 69.9, 74.5, 75.2, 65.3, 55.8, 49.2, 40.9,
    33.4, 38.1, 39.6, 55.0, 57.2, 73.0, 77.8, 76.2, 70.7, 55.0, 41.6, 31.8,
    24.6, 27.0, 43.0, 52.7, 61.1, 66.6, 73.2, 73.7, 65.7, 52.3, 49.0, 33.7,
    26.7, 29.1, 42.3, 52.1, 65.8, 69.7, 73.0, 70.8, 68.5, 55.0, 48.7, 32.4,
    32.7, 33.3, 34.9, 54.8, 57.4, 72.7, 76.8, 76.4, 71.0, 56.3, 45.7, 30.3)

year <- seq(from=1995, by=1/12, length=length(temp))

## seasonal component carried over from the Deseasoning task
d <- 12
n <- length(temp)
our.filter <- c(0.5, rep(1,d-1), 0.5)/d
longer.temp <- c(temp[(d/2):1], temp, temp[n:(n-d/2+1)])
temp.MA <- filter(longer.temp, our.filter)[(d/2+1):(d/2+n)]
temp.matrix <- matrix(temp - temp.MA, nrow=d)
s.hat <- rowMeans(temp.matrix) - mean(rowMeans(temp.matrix))
deseasonalized <- temp - s.hat

## fit a quadratic trend to the deseasonalized data and take residuals
lm.deseasonalized <- lm(deseasonalized ~ year + I(year^2))
resids <- resid(lm.deseasonalized)
par(mfrow=c(2, 2))

## Plot the residual series with a horizontal line at y=0.
plot(year, resids, type="l", xlab="year", ylab="estimate of noise process")
abline(h=0, lty=2)

## plot the acf
acf(resids, main="")

## carry out the Ljung-Box test
Box.test(resids, 22, type="Ljung-Box")


    Box-Ljung test

data:  resids
X-squared = 25.455, df = 22, p-value = 0.2758

## draw a Q-Q plot of the residuals.
qqnorm(resids, xlab="z-scores", ylab="estimate of noise process", main="")
qqline(resids)
The residual series shows no remaining trend or seasonality; the Ljung-Box test fails to reject IID noise (p ≈ 0.28); and the Q-Q plot supports normality.

title: Deseasoning
text:
NoteIntroduction



Use classical decomposition to estimate a series’ seasonal component and subtract it, leaving a deseasonalized series.
The classical decomposition algorithm (CDA) applied to a time series of monthly temperatures recorded in Columbus, OH from January 1995 to December 2005 (degrees Fahrenheit).
temp <-
  c(29.9, 28.1, 44.3, 50.6, 61.0, 72.4, 75.6, 76.9, 63.6, 55.7, 38.1, 29.2,
    27.9, 30.7, 34.4, 50.5, 60.6, 70.8, 73.9, 73.6, 65.8, 55.5, 37.3, 37.4,
    28.0, 35.9, 42.9, 47.9, 57.1, 70.0, 74.3, 70.4, 65.4, 55.3, 40.6, 35.4,
    37.0, 39.4, 42.9, 53.7, 67.2, 71.7, 74.9, 75.7, 71.5, 56.0, 46.0, 41.5,
    31.3, 36.2, 36.9, 54.7, 64.8, 74.5, 79.2, 73.7, 68.3, 54.7, 48.6, 34.6,
    28.0, 36.5, 46.5, 50.5, 64.5, 71.7, 71.9, 71.3, 65.8, 57.5, 42.1, 24.4,
    27.6, 35.9, 37.1, 55.8, 63.3, 69.9, 74.5, 75.2, 65.3, 55.8, 49.2, 40.9,
    33.4, 38.1, 39.6, 55.0, 57.2, 73.0, 77.8, 76.2, 70.7, 55.0, 41.6, 31.8,
    24.6, 27.0, 43.0, 52.7, 61.1, 66.6, 73.2, 73.7, 65.7, 52.3, 49.0, 33.7,
    26.7, 29.1, 42.3, 52.1, 65.8, 69.7, 73.0, 70.8, 68.5, 55.0, 48.7, 32.4,
    32.7, 33.3, 34.9, 54.8, 57.4, 72.7, 76.8, 76.4, 71.0, 56.3, 45.7, 30.3)

year <- seq(from=1995, by=1/12, length=length(temp))
Step 1 — estimate the trend by smoothing the series with a moving-average filter over a d = 12 month period.
d <- 12
half.d <- d/2

## smooth the temp time series using the 'filter' command
our.filter <- c(0.5, rep(1,d-1), 0.5)/d
n <- length(temp)

## filter a padded copy so the smooth spans the original years
longer.temp <- c(temp[(d/2):1], temp, temp[n:(n-d/2+1)])
temp.MA <- filter(longer.temp, our.filter)[(d/2+1):(d/2+n)]

plot(year, temp, type="l",
     xlab="year", ylab="monthly temperature (Fahrenheit)")
lines(year, temp.MA, type="l", lwd=2)
Step 2 — estimate the seasonal component from the residuals of the smooth.
## residuals of the smooth
temp.MA.resids <- temp - temp.MA

months <- c("Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
            "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec")

## form the residuals into a matrix with 'd' rows and average across years
temp.matrix <- matrix(temp.MA.resids, nrow=d)
s.hat.stars <- rowMeans(temp.matrix)

## subtract the mean so the seasonal component sums to zero
s.hat <- s.hat.stars - mean(s.hat.stars)

plot(s.hat, type="l", xaxt="n",
     xlab="month", ylab="estimate of seasonal component")
axis(side=1, at=1:12, labels=months)
Step 3 — deseasonalize by subtracting the seasonal component.
deseasonalized <- temp - s.hat

plot(year, deseasonalized, type="l",
     xlab="year", ylab="deseasonalized temperature")

  row_to_names(row_number = 1)

NoteIntroduction



A cautionary example: with empty cells and too few observations, some factorial effects simply cannot be estimated.

title: Sample Size

NoteIntroduction



Use a power calculation to find how many observations per treatment you need to detect a given effect size.
pacman::p_load(pwr)

# sigma2 carried over from the Conservative Estimate of Variance task
chi <- qchisq(.95, 36, lower.tail = FALSE)
sse <- 56.471
sigma2 <- sse/chi

delta <- 1.5                                            #given delta
v <- 4                                                  #treatment groups
#sigma2 from conservative estimate above
f <- sqrt((delta^2)/(2*v*sigma2))                       #effect size
pwr.anova.test(4,
               sig.level=.05,
               power = .95,                             #desired power
               f=f)

      sig.level = 0.05
          power = 0.95

# read data
fish <- read_table('../../data/fish.txt')

# function for population size
N_hat <- function(df){
  fitted_model <- lm(I(1/R) ~ I(1/S), data = df)
  beta <- unname(coef(fitted_model))
  (1 - beta[2]) / beta[1]
}

# number of bootstrap replicates
n_bootstrap_replicates <- 1e3

# create bootstrap samples
fish_boot <- map(1:n_bootstrap_replicates,
                 function(x) slice_sample(fish,
                                          n = nrow(fish),
                                          replace = TRUE)
                 )

# evaluate n_hat on samples
N_hat_boot <- map_dbl(fish_boot, N_hat)

# find standard error of result
(N_hat_se <- sd(N_hat_boot))

[1] 3.868415

# store linear models fit to each bootstrap
linear_models <- map(fish_boot, function(x) lm(I(1/R) ~ I(1/S), data = x))

# store coefficients of linear models
coef_boot <- map(linear_models, coef)

# function to estimate R from S
bh_curve <- function(beta, S) {
  tibble(
    S = S,
    R = 1 / (beta[1] + (beta[2] / S))
  )
}

# create curve using betas from coef_boot
bh_boot <- map(coef_boot, bh_curve, S = seq(50,500,by=1))

# bind rows and store list # in id column
bh_boot_df <- bind_rows(bh_boot, .id = "bootstrap_replicate")

# plot the curves
ggplot(fish, mapping = aes(x = S, y = R)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line(
    data = bh_boot_df,
    mapping = aes(group = bootstrap_replicate),
    alpha = 1 / 20
  )

title: Multiple Logistic Regression
text:
NoteIntroduction



Use multiple logistic regression to model a binary outcome from several predictors, interpreting coefficients as odds ratios.
A local health clinic sent fliers to its clients to encourage everyone, but especially older persons at high risk of complications, to get a flu shot in time for protection against an expected flu epidemic. In a pilot follow-up study, 159 clients were randomly selected and asked whether they actually received a flu shot. A client who received a flu shot was coded \(Y=1\), and a client who did not receive a flu shot was coded \(Y=0\). In addition, data were collected on their age (\(X_1\)), and their health awareness. The latter data were combined into a health awareness index (\(X_2\)), for which higher values indicate greater awareness. Also included in the dta was client gender, where males were coded \(X_3=1\) and females were coded \(X_3=0\).
Multiple logistic regression model with three predictor variables in first- order terms is assumed to be appropriate.
# read data
vaxx <- read.table("../../data/CH14PR14.txt", header=TRUE)

# fit logistic model
gfit3 <- glm(Y ~ X1 + X2 + X3, data=vaxx, family = binomial(link ="logit"))
summary(gfit3)

    data = vaxx)

It is expected that when all other variables are held constant, one unit increase in age increases the probability of having gotten a flu shot by 7.55%.
It is expected that when all other variables are held constant, one unit increase in a person’s health awareness score decreased the probability of them having gotten a flu shot by nearly 10%.
It is expected that when all other variables are held constant, a man is 54% more likely to have gotten a flu shot than a woman with the same age and health awareness.

title: Added Variable Plot
text:
NoteIntroduction



Use an added-variable (partial regression) plot to judge whether a predictor still contributes once the other predictors are already in the model.
# required package
library(car)

# patient data carried over from the Multiple Linear Regression task
patient <- read.table("../../data/CH06PR15.txt", header=TRUE)

# fit linear model
gfit2 <- lm(Satisfaction ~ Age + Severity + Anxiety, data=patient)

# added variable plot
avPlots(gfit2)
Upon visual inspection of the added variable plots, it appears that \(X_1\), which represents age, may have a linear relationship with patient satisfaction, while the relationship between \(X_2\) and \(X_3\) and patient satisfaction remains unclear. It is possible these variables do not significantly add value to the model.

title: ANOVA Table
text:
NoteIntroduction



Use the ANOVA table to partition sums of squares and F-test whether the predictors collectively improve on an intercept-only model.
# read data
gpa <- read.table("../../data/CH03PR03.txt", header=TRUE)

# fit linear model (defined when checking assumptions on this data)
gfit <- lm(Y ~ X, data=gpa)

# anova table
anova(gfit)

title: ANOVA Table
text:
Answers

F Test
Null Hypothesis: \(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\)
Alternate Hypothesis: \(H_a: \beta_1 \neq 0\)
Because the test statistic is larger than F*, I can reject the null hypothesis with 95% confidence. I conclude that the full regression model is better than an intercept-only model.


Unknown Test????
\(H_0: \hat{Y} = -0.3240X + 2.5753\); \(H_a: \hat{Y} \neq -0.3240X + 2.5753\)
This information does not indicate what regression function is appropriate, since all other regression functions are included in the alternate hypothesis. If we want to explore alternate regression functions, we can look to other diagnostics and examine the residuals.
